messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: design_manual_pages_12_13_14_16_17_18_22.pdf
Equipment class: pump

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

{"candidates": [{"candidate_id": "cand_98585cd87efc61e2", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Design Process Phases", "summary": "The design phase is broken into conceptual design, schematic design, and design development subphases.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Design Process Phases", "summary": "The design phase is broken into conceptual design, schematic design, and design development subphases."}, "evidence": "92.1 DESIGN PROCESS CONTEXT
There are numerous variations of  the design process, perhaps
as many as there are designers. To  try and place the following infor-
mation into a common context, the design process structure used in
ASHRAE Guideline 0-2005, The Commissioning Process
(ASHRAE 2005a) will be used. Fo r purposes of building commis-
sioning, the acquisition of a build ing is assumed to flow through
several broad phases: predesign, design, construction, and occu-
pancy and operation. The design phase is often broken into concep-
tual design, schematic design, a nd design development subphases.
Although the majority of design hours will be spent in the design
development phase, each of these phases plays a critical role in a
successful building project. Each ph ase should have input from the
HV AC&R design team.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_41b356fa1f3403d3", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Owner's Project Requirements (OPR)", "summary": "Design should start with a clear statement of design intent, forming the Owner's Project Requirements (OPR) document.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Owner's Project Requirements (OPR)", "summary": "Design should start with a clear statement of design intent, forming the Owner's Project Requirements (OPR) document."}, "evidence": "The HV AC&R design team should strive to
provide input during the earlie st phases (when HV AC&R design
input has historically been minimal) since these are the most critical
to project success, as they set the stage for all subsequent work.
Design should start with a clear statement of design intent . In
commissioning terms, the collective project intents form the
Owner’s Project Requirements (OPR) document. Intent is simply a
declaration of the owner’s (and design team’s) needs and wants in
terms of project outcomes. HV AC&R design intents might include
exceptional energy efficiency, acceptable indoor air quality, low
maintenance, high flexibility, and the like. Each design intent must
be paired with a design criterion , which provides a benchmark for
minimum acceptable performance rela tive to the intent.", "source_pages": [1], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_36f9b5e654fff15c", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Design Intent and Criterion", "summary": "Each design intent must be paired with a design criterion providing a benchmark for minimum acceptable performance.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Design Intent and Criterion", "summary": "Each design intent must be paired with a design criterion providing a benchmark for minimum acceptable performance."}, "evidence": "The HV AC&R design team should strive to

input has historically been minimal) since these are the most critical
to project success, as they set the stage for all subsequent work.

pancy and operation. The design phase is often broken into concep-

There are numerous variations of  the design process, perhaps
as many as there are designers. To  try and place the following infor-
mation into a common context, the design process structure used in
ASHRAE Guideline 0-2005, The Commissioning Process
(ASHRAE 2005a) will be used. Fo r purposes of building commis-

pancy and operation. The design phase is often broken into concep-

Although the majority of design hours will be spent in the design

successful building project. Each ph ase should have input from the
HV AC&R design team.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_d140fe48dac5becf", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Iterative Design Process", "summary": "Design is an iterative process with continuous back-and-forth as the designer selects from available options.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Iterative Design Process", "summary": "Design is an iterative process with continuous back-and-forth as the designer selects from available options."}, "evidence": "If you are
used to looking for the correct answer to a problem (via analysis),
and are suddenly faced with problems that have several acceptable
answers (via design), how do you decide which solution to select?
Learn to use your judgment (or the advice of experienced col-
leagues) to weigh the merits of a number of solutions that seem to
work for a particular design prob lem in order to select the best
among them.
Figures 2-1 and 2-2 illustrate the analysis and design pro-
cesses, respectively. Analysis proc eeds in a generally unidirectional
flow from given data to final answer with the aid of certain analyti-
cal tools. Design, however, is an  iterative process. Although there
are certain “givens” to start with , they are often not immutable but.", "source_pages": [2], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_fc82b0c17c6fbc97", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Predesign Phase Building Program", "summary": "The building program includes client objectives, space functions, occupant patterns, equipment loads, site location, codes, budget, schedule, and quality.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Predesign Phase Building Program", "summary": "The building program includes client objectives, space functions, occupant patterns, equipment loads, site location, codes, budget, schedule, and quality."}, "evidence": "The building program should
include, but need not be  limited to, the following:
 The client’s objectives and strategies for the initial and
future functional use of the building, whether it be a single-
or multiple-family dwelling or a commercial, industrial, ath-
letic, or other facility.
 A clear description of function(s)  for each discrete area within
the building.
 The number, distribution, and usage patterns of permanent
occupants and visitors.
 The type, distribution, and usage patterns of owner-provided
heat-producing equipment.
 The geographic site location, access means, and applicable
building and zoning codes.
 The proposed building area, he ight, number of stories, and
mechanized circulation requirements.
 The owner’s capital cost and operating cost budgets.
 A clear statement of anticipated  project schedule and/or time
constraints.
 A clear statement of required or expected project quality.
Although some of this informa tion may not be available before
the mechanical designer starts to work, it must be obtained as soon
as possible to ensure that only those HV AC&R systems that are
compatible with the building program are considered.
While the architect prepares the general building program, the
mechanical engineer has the responsibility of developing a disci-
pline-specific program even though  some of this information may
be provided by the architect or owner.", "source_pages": [4], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_7057579d5b77f254", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "HVAC&R System Program Content", "summary": "HVAC&R system program includes design temperatures, degree-days, wind velocity, codes, and utility characteristics.", "structured_payload": {"title": "HVAC&R System Program Content", "summary": "HVAC&R system program includes design temperatures, degree-days, wind velocity, codes, and utility characteristics."}, "evidence": "92.1 DESIGN PROCESS CONTEXT

as many as there are designers. To  try and place the following infor-
mation into a common context, the design process structure used in
ASHRAE Guideline 0-2005, The Commissioning Process
(ASHRAE 2005a) will be used. Fo r purposes of building commis-
sioning, the acquisition of a build ing is assumed to flow through
several broad phases: predesign, design, construction, and occu-
pancy and operation. The design phase is often broken into concep-
tual design, schematic design, a nd design development subphases.
Although the majority of design hours will be spent in the design
development phase, each of these phases plays a critical role in a
successful building project. Each ph ase should have input from the
HV AC&R design team.", "source_pages": [1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_532933fd46ff2a71", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Environmental Conditions Definition", "summary": "Environmental conditions to be maintained include dry-bulb/wet-bulb temperatures, ventilation, special loads, and acceptable ranges.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Environmental Conditions Definition", "summary": "Environmental conditions to be maintained include dry-bulb/wet-bulb temperatures, ventilation, special loads, and acceptable ranges."}, "evidence": "For exam-

a criterion that requires compliance with ANSI/ASHRAE Standard

pancy  (ASHRAE 2004b), and an intent for energy efficiency mightCHAPTER  2
THE DESIGN  PROCESS.", "source_pages": [1, 3, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_c1b7002bbe82abae", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Schematic Design Evaluation Criteria", "summary": "Evaluate space requirements, energy use, costs, acoustics, compatibility, IAQ, and aesthetics for each system.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Schematic Design Evaluation Criteria", "summary": "Evaluate space requirements, energy use, costs, acoustics, compatibility, IAQ, and aesthetics for each system."}, "evidence": "92.1 DESIGN PROCESS CONTEXT

as many as there are designers. To  try and place the following infor-
mation into a common context, the design process structure used in

pancy and operation. The design phase is often broken into concep-

Although the majority of design hours will be spent in the design
development phase, each of these phases plays a critical role in a
successful building project. Each ph ase should have input from the
HV AC&R design team.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_232be5db2866c9bc", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Coordination with Other Disciplines", "summary": "Schematic HVAC design coordinates with architectural, structural, electrical, and fire protection systems.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Coordination with Other Disciplines", "summary": "Schematic HVAC design coordinates with architectural, structural, electrical, and fire protection systems."}, "evidence": "AIR-CONDITIONING  SYSTEM  DESIGN  MANUAL ⏐19
owner's prerogative. Design de cisions should be clearly docu-
mented whether they constitute appr oval of, or deviation from, the
designer's proposals.
The schematic HV AC design is used to coordinate critical
HV AC&R system requirements with the architectural, structural,
electrical, and fire protection systems, which are also in the sche-
matic design stage, to resolve po tential conflicts. Close integration
of the mechanical and electrical systems with structure, plan, and
building configuration requires th e cooperation of all team mem-
bers—architect, mechanical engineer, electrical engineer, structural
engineer, acoustical consultant, and professionals from other disci-
plines.", "source_pages": [7], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_c556a53f1f7a59f6", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Energy Code Compliance", "summary": "State or local building or energy codes must be considered, including restrictions on glass, envelope, lighting, and HVAC systems.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Energy Code Compliance", "summary": "State or local building or energy codes must be considered, including restrictions on glass, envelope, lighting, and HVAC systems."}, "evidence": "The HV AC&R design team should strive to
provide input during the earlie st phases (when HV AC&R design
input has historically been minimal) since these are the most critical
to project success, as they set the stage for all subsequent work.
Design should start with a clear statement of design intent . In
commissioning terms, the collective project intents form the
Owner’s Project Requirements (OPR) document. Intent is simply a
declaration of the owner’s (and design team’s) needs and wants in
terms of project outcomes. HV AC&R design intents might include
exceptional energy efficiency, acceptable indoor air quality, low
maintenance, high flexibility, and the like. Each design intent must
be paired with a design criterion , which provides a benchmark for
minimum acceptable performance rela tive to the intent.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_3f2c86664aa98225", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Design Development Calculations", "summary": "Heat loss, heat gain, and ventilation calculations are refined to design air and water distribution systems and select equipment.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Design Development Calculations", "summary": "Heat loss, heat gain, and ventilation calculations are refined to design air and water distribution systems and select equipment."}, "evidence": "Programs are available
that share input between lo ads and energy programs.
Architectural floor plans and elevations are developed in
greater detail; structural, mechanical, and electrical systems are
designed in compliance with applicable building codes; and draw-
ings in preliminary form are prepared. Heat loss, heat gain, and
ventilation calculations are refined and used to design the air and
water distribution systems and to select equipment. System sizes
and capacities are selected to ma tch design and part-load condi-
tions. The designer has the choice of  sizing pipes and ducts manu-
ally or using a variety of computer programs. The main objective,
however, is to develop system layouts for space requirements and
cost estimates. If required by code  or owner intent, more detailed
energy studies are undertaken at this time and the accompanying
calculations are performed. Construction details and cost estimates
are refined based upon additional information.", "source_pages": [7], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_4180a8d7de549ed3", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Owner Approval of Preliminary Design", "summary": "After preliminary drawings, outline specifications, and cost estimates are approved by the owner, the project scope is finalized.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Owner Approval of Preliminary Design", "summary": "After preliminary drawings, outline specifications, and cost estimates are approved by the owner, the project scope is finalized."}, "evidence": "92.1 DESIGN PROCESS CONTEXT
There are numerous variations of  the design process, perhaps
as many as there are designers. To  try and place the following infor-
mation into a common context, the design process structure used in
ASHRAE Guideline 0-2005, The Commissioning Process
(ASHRAE 2005a) will be used. Fo r purposes of building commis-
sioning, the acquisition of a build ing is assumed to flow through
several broad phases: predesign, design, construction, and occu-
pancy and operation. The design phase is often broken into concep-
tual design, schematic design, a nd design development subphases.
Although the majority of design hours will be spent in the design
development phase, each of these phases plays a critical role in a
successful building project. Each ph ase should have input from the
HV AC&R design team.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], "trust_level": "L4"}]}
